howItWorks.intro: This vet bill estimator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Base cost adjusted by pet size, clinic type, and region
Cats typically cost 20% less than dogs for comparable procedures
Specialty and emergency clinics charge 60-80% more than general practice
High-cost cities (NYC, SF, LA) add 40-50% to average costs
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates. Consult a professional for critical decisions.

faq.question: How much does a vet visit cost?
faq.answer: A routine wellness exam costs $50-$125 at a general practice. Add vaccinations ($75-$200), heartworm test ($25-$50), and fecal exam ($25-$50) for a typical annual visit total of $175-$400. Emergency vet visits start at $150-$300 just for the exam fee, with diagnostics and treatment often running $500-$3,000+. After-hours emergency clinics typically charge 50-100% more than regular-hours practices.

faq.question: How much does dog dental cleaning cost?
faq.answer: Professional dental cleaning under anesthesia costs $300-$800 at general practices and $600-$1,500 at specialty clinics. This includes anesthesia, scaling, polishing, and dental X-rays. Tooth extractions add $50-$300 per tooth depending on difficulty. Most dogs need their first dental cleaning by age 3-4, with annual cleanings recommended afterward. Dental disease affects 80% of dogs by age 3 and can lead to serious health problems if untreated.

faq.question: Why are emergency vet visits so expensive?
faq.answer: Emergency vet clinics charge premium prices because they maintain 24/7 staffing, specialized equipment, and on-call specialists. The exam fee alone is typically $150-$300 versus $50-$100 at regular clinics. Add diagnostics ($200-$800), treatment ($300-$3,000+), and possible hospitalization ($800-$2,000/night), and a single emergency visit often costs $1,500-$5,000. Having a relationship with a regular vet who offers urgent-care hours can save significantly.

faq.question: How can I reduce vet costs without compromising care?
faq.answer: Smart strategies include: keep up with preventive care (vaccines, dental, parasite prevention) to avoid expensive problems, use low-cost vaccination clinics for routine shots ($15-$25/vaccine), ask about payment plans — most vets offer CareCredit or Scratchpay, get estimates before procedures and ask about alternatives, consider veterinary schools for complex procedures (40-60% less), and buy medications online (Chewy, PetCareRx) instead of at the vet office where markup is 100-200%.
